Paul writes to a church fragmenting from within, and his words strike like a gavel. The Corinthians are dragging each other into pagan courts, airing their disputes before unbelievers, and Paul doesn't soften the rebuke with gentle language. He shames them. He asks—rhetorically, cutting—where is the wisdom among you? Where is the one capable of settling a matter between brothers? The question isn't really a question. It's an indictment. They have been given an extraordinary destiny: the saints will judge the world, will judge angels. Yet they cannot manage the trivial disputes of daily life. They have traded their future glory for the immediate satisfaction of winning in court. Worse still, in doing so, they have already lost. The very fact that they are locked in lawsuits means they are defeated—not by their opponent, but by their own refusal to absorb injustice. Paul invites them to a radical possibility: Why not rather be wronged? Why not rather be cheated? This is not passive resignation. It is the confidence of someone who knows that all judgment ultimately belongs to God, and that vindication will come from Him, not from earthly courts.

But Paul doesn't stop with disputes over property and money. He pivots to something deeper—the sins that the Corinthians are apparently glossing over as morally neutral, and he lists them with unflinching clarity: the sexually immoral, adulterers, those engaged in homosexual acts, thieves, the greedy, drunkards, slanderers, swindlers. None of these, he declares, will inherit the kingdom of God. The list should land hard. These are not abstract vices; they are the actual behaviors the Corinthians are struggling with, the temptations they are rationalizing, perhaps even celebrating as expressions of freedom. Yet Paul will not allow them to deceive themselves. What we do with our bodies matters infinitely. Our actions are not morally neutral. But then, in one of Scripture's most liberating turns, Paul reminds them of who they actually are: "And that is what some of you were." Past tense. They were washed. They were sanctified. They were justified. In the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of God, their identities have been fundamentally rewritten. This is the gospel breaking into their self-justification.

The Corinthians are operating under a slogan—"Everything is permissible for me"—and Paul doesn't deny the technical freedom they possess as believers. But freedom, he insists, is not the highest good. Not everything that is permissible is beneficial, and not everything is worth the cost of being mastered by it. Then he addresses sexual immorality directly because it touches something the other sins do not: your body is a member of Christ. When you unite yourself with a prostitute, you are joining Christ Himself to her. This is not metaphor or exaggeration. Paul appeals to the physical reality: the two become one flesh. But when you unite yourself with the Lord, you become one with Him in spirit. There is a union with Christ that transcends the temporary union of bodies. Your body, Paul reminds them, is a temple of the Holy Spirit—not your private possession to do with as you wish, but the dwelling place of God Himself. You were bought at a price. Redemption means you are no longer your own. Every sexual choice, every use of your body, is either glorifying God or desecrating His temple. This is the weight of embodied Christian life that Paul refuses to let them ignore.
